meta.title: AI Exposure of the Canadian Job Market
meta.primary_rollup_note: The dashboard rolls canonical NOC 2021 unit groups through the 45 official major groups.
meta.metric_model_note: StatCan annual labour-force and wage tables are published at a coarser occupation grain than official NOC 2021 unit groups, so the unit-group labour metrics are allocated using ESDC unit employment weights. Outlook remains direct from the ESDC unit-group release.
meta.exposure_metric_scale: 0-10 display score derived from the share of workers in high-exposure EPIAC occupations
meta.exposure_note: Official EPIAC fields are mapped from published StatCan occupation groups onto the canonical NOC 2021 unit groups.
meta.profile_layer_note: Official OaSIS occupational profiles attach to canonical NOC 2021 unit groups through an explicit generated mapping table.
meta.source: Statistics Canada annual occupation tables rebuilt onto the canonical NOC 2021 spine

oasis.title: Occupational and Skills Information System (OaSIS) - 2025 Version 1.0

family.title: Occupations in natural resources and related production
family.category: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
family.definition: This major group comprises occupations in natural resources and related production. These occupations come with Training, Education, Experience and Responsibility (TEER) identified as TEER 3. Occupations classified in TEER 3, as those included in this group with a 3 as the second digit of their code, typically require completion of a post-secondary education program of less than two years at community college, institute of technology or CÉGEP; or completion of an apprenticeship training program of less than two years; or more than six months of on-the-job training, training courses or specific work experience with some secondary school education; or the accumulation of several years of experience in a related occupation found in TEER 4, when applicable (TEER 4 includes occupations with a 4 as a second digit in their code).
family.broad_category_title: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
family.major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and related production
family.exposure_rationale: Aggregated from canonical unit groups. EPIAC values roll up unit-level mappings from published StatCan occupation groups.
family.metric_rationale: This major group aggregates canonical unit groups. Labour-market counts and wages reflect the unit-group estimates allocated from published StatCan annual occupation tables using ESDC employment weights.
family.epiac_source_title: Experimental Estimates of Potential Artificial Intelligence Occupational Exposure in Canada, 2024
epiac_source_groups: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations (8)

units.title: Underground production and development miners
units.category: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.broad_category_title: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and related production
units.sub_major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and fisheries
units.minor_group_title: Underground miners, oil and gas drillers and related occupations
units.definition: Underground production and development miners drill, blast, operate mining machinery, and perform related duties to extract coal and ore in underground mines and to construct tunnels, passageways and shafts to facilitate mining operations. They are employed by coal, metal and non-metallic mineral underground mines and by specialized contractors in mine construction, shaft sinking and tunnelling. Apprentices are also included in this unit group.
units.metric_source_note: StatCan annual labour-force and wage tables do not publish this official NOC 2021 unit group directly. Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.
units.metric_rationale: StatCan annual labour-force and wage tables do not publish this official NOC 2021 unit group directly. Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.
units.exposure_rationale: Mapped to the closest published StatCan EPIAC occupation group from the 2021 Census (NOC 2016): Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations (8).
units.epiac_source_title: Experimental Estimates of Potential Artificial Intelligence Occupational Exposure in Canada, 2024
epiac_source_groups: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations (8)

oasis_profiles.lead_statement: Underground production and development miners drill, blast, operate mining machinery, and perform related duties to extract coal and ore in underground mines and to construct tunnels, passageways and shafts to facilitate mining operations. Apprentices are also included in this unit group.
employment_requirements: May be certified in the basic common core program or as an underground hard rock miner in Ontario.
Certificate in first aid may be required.
Formal training of up to six weeks followed by extended periods of specialized training as a helper or in support occupations is usually provided.
Company licensing or certification is often required for occupations in this unit group.
Previous experience as a mine labourer or in other mine occupations is usually required.
Provincial blasting licence may be required.
Trade certification for miners is available, but voluntary, in Quebec and Manitoba.
Completion of secondary school is usually required.
additional_information: Mobility is possible to other occupational groups in underground mining such as underground service and support occupations.
Mobility between these sectors is somewhat limited by differences in production technologies.
There is mobility between employers within each of the three following sectors: underground coal mining, underground hard rock mining and underground potash, salt or soft rock mining.
Progression to mining supervisor is possible with experience.
workplaces_employers: Coal, metal and non-metallic mineral underground mines
Specialized contractors in mine construction, shaft sinking and tunnelling

example_titles.job_title_text: jack leg drill operator - underground mining
raise boring machine operator - underground mining

core_competencies.statement: to ensure equipment performs optimally, reducing the risk of breakdowns and improving the overall productivity of the mining operation

core_competencies.competency: Equipment and Tool Selection, and Setting Up
core_competencies.statement: to select the appropriate drilling machines or explosives to blast and remove ore, coal or rock from the working location
to adjust settings of drilling and blasting equipment to suit varying rock conditions and mining requirements
to respond quickly to sudden changes, such as equipment malfunctions, gas leaks or unexpected geological conditions
to carefully follow procedures, monitor equipment conditions and inspect work areas to identify any issues or hazards that could affect the mining process
to determine the most effective techniques for mining specific resources and assess potential hazards, such as unstable rock formations or gas pockets

units.title: Oil and gas well drillers, servicers, testers and related workers
units.category: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.broad_category_title: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and related production
units.sub_major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and fisheries
units.minor_group_title: Underground miners, oil and gas drillers and related occupations
units.definition: Oil and gas well drillers and well servicers control the operation of drilling and hoisting equipment on drilling and service rigs, and direct the activities of the rig crew under supervision of the rig manager. Oil and gas well loggers, testers and related workers operate specialized mechanical or electronic equipment, tools or instruments to provide services in conjunction with well drilling, completion or servicing. They are employed by drilling and well service contractors, petroleum producing companies and well logging or testing companies.
units.metric_source_note: StatCan annual labour-force and wage tables do not publish this official NOC 2021 unit group directly. Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.
units.metric_rationale: StatCan annual labour-force and wage tables do not publish this official NOC 2021 unit group directly. Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.

oasis_profiles.profile_title: Oil and gas well drillers and well servicers
oasis_profiles.unit_group_title: Oil and gas well drillers, servicers, testers and related workers
oasis_profiles.lead_statement: Oil and gas well drillers and well servicers control the operation of drilling and hoisting equipment on drilling and service rigs, and direct the activities of the rig crew under supervision of the rig manager.
main_duties: Oil and gas well drillers and well servicers
Direct rig crew in setting up rigs and drilling, completing or servicing oil and gas exploration and producing wells
Operate controls of drill or service rig drilling and hoisting machinery
Train or arrange for training of crew
Maintain records of drilling and servicing operations
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.

Oil and gas well drillers and well servicers require three to six months of formal on-the-job training, college or petroleum industry-approved training courses and four or more years of work experience in subordinate rig crew positions.
A college diploma in drilling may be required.
Offshore work requires several years of experience in an equivalent position on land.

Certificates in first aid, hydrogen sulphide awareness, blowout prevention, well control, workplace hazardous materials information system (WHMIS), transportation of dangerous goods (TDG) and a special oil well operator (boiler) certificate are required.
A provincial blaster's licence, and seismic blaster and oil well blaster certification are required for well perforation services.

Trade certification for rig technician is compulsory in Alberta and available, but voluntary, in Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, Manitoba, Saskatchewan, British Columbia and the Northwest Territories.
Red Seal endorsement is also available to qualified rig technicians upon successful completion of the interprovincial Red Seal examination.
additional_information: Mobility between jobs on drilling and service rigs is limited by the differences in the machinery, tools and operations performed, but mobility is possible especially from drilling to service rigs.
Progression to crew supervisor or manager is possible with experience and additional education and training.
The Red Seal endorsement allows for interprovincial mobility.

oasis_profiles.profile_title: Oil and gas well loggers, testers and related workers
oasis_profiles.unit_group_title: Oil and gas well drillers, servicers, testers and related workers
oasis_profiles.lead_statement: Oil and gas well loggers, testers and related workers operate specialized mechanical or electronic equipment, tools or instruments to provide services in conjunction with well drilling, completion or servicing.
main_duties: Oil and gas well loggers, testers and related workers
Drive well service or wireline truck to well site
Assemble and attach equipment, tools or recorders to drill stem or wireline to conduct required procedures and tests
Operate or direct the operation of wireline or unit controls to lower, position and retrieve equipment and instruments
Operate recorders and computers in mobile testing or logging unit to collect data
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
Offshore work requires several years of experience in an equivalent position on land.

Certificates in first aid, hydrogen sulphide awareness, blowout prevention, well control, workplace hazardous materials information system (WHMIS), transportation of dangerous goods (TDG) and a special oil well operator (boiler) certificate are required.

Oil and gas well loggers, testers and related workers require three to six months of formal on-the-job training and several years of experience in subordinate logging and testing positions or on drilling and servicing rigs.
Completion of a college program in electronics or engineering technology may be required for open hole well logging.
additional_information: Mobility between jobs on drilling and service rigs is limited by the differences in the machinery, tools and operations performed, but mobility is possible especially from drilling to service rigs.
Progression to crew supervisor or manager is possible with experience and additional education and training.
The Red Seal endorsement allows for interprovincial mobility.

example_titles.job_title_text: drill stem tester - oil and gas drilling
logging and perforating operator - oil field services
service rig operator - oil field services
well testing operator - oil and gas drilling

units.title: Logging machinery operators
units.category: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.broad_category_title: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and related production
units.sub_major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and fisheries
units.definition: Logging machinery operators operate cable yarding systems, mechanical harvesters and forwarders and mechanical tree processors and loaders to fell, yard and process trees at logging sites. They are employed by logging companies and contractors.
units.metric_source_note: StatCan annual labour-force and wage tables do not publish this official NOC 2021 unit group directly. Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.

Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.

epiac_source_groups: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations (8)

oasis_profiles.lead_statement: Logging machinery operators operate cable yarding systems, mechanical harvesters and forwarders and mechanical tree processors and loaders to fell, yard and process trees at logging sites.
employment_requirements: Feller buncher operators and cable yarder operators usually require three to five years of logging experience.
Mechanical tree processor and loader operators usually require one to three years of logging experience.
Company certification for mobile logging machinery operation may be required.
Workplace hazardous materials information system (WHMIS) and first aid certificates may be required.
Completion of secondary school may be required.
Mechanical harvester and forwarder operators may require logging experience as a chain saw and skidder operator.
Experience requirements vary depending on the complexity of machinery operated.
Certification as a heavy equipment operator may be required.
Provincial air brake certification may be required in British Columbia, depending on the type of equipment operated.
Knowledge of tree harvesting regulations is required.
On-the-job training from three to 16 months is provided, depending on the complexity of machinery operated and the type of woodlands operation.
additional_information: Self-employment as a logging contractor is possible with investment in equipment.
There is some mobility among jobs in this group from the less complex to more complex machinery operation.
There is some mobility between employers, particularly in similar types of woodland operations.
Logging machinery operators may perform minor repairs and maintenance on their equipment.
Progression to logging and forestry supervisory positions is possible with experience.

example_titles.job_title_text: mechanical tree processor and loader operator

core_competencies.competency: Operation Monitoring of Machinery and Equipment
core_competencies.statement: to oversee machinery operations to detect performance issues early and ensure timely repairs
to use machinery to transport and process trees efficiently and safely
to adjust machinery settings to adapt to varying conditions and achieve accurate results in logging operations
to quickly respond to issues, such as equipment malfunctions or falling debris to minimize risks and reduce operational delays
to work efficiently in remote locations, make quick decisions and solve problems without constant supervision

core_competencies.competency: Knowledge in Vehicle, Machinery and Equipment Operations
core_competencies.statement: to operate and handle logging equipment efficiently, address issues promptly and ensure optimal performance

units.title: Fishing masters and officers
units.category: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.broad_category_title: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and related production
units.sub_major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and fisheries
units.definition: Fishing masters and officers manage and operate saltwater and freshwater fishing vessels greater than 100 gross tonnes to pursue and land fish and other marine life. They are employed by establishments engaged in operating commercial fishing vessels.
units.metric_source_note: StatCan annual labour-force and wage tables do not publish this official NOC 2021 unit group directly. Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.

Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.

epiac_source_groups: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations (8)

oasis_profiles.lead_statement: Fishing masters and officers manage and operate saltwater and freshwater fishing vessels greater than 100 gross tonnes to pursue and land fish and other marine life.
employment_requirements: Some secondary school education is usually required.
One to two years of experience as a fishing vessel deckhand is required.
Captains require one year of service as officer in charge of the watch.
A Fishing Master's Certificate is required for all occupations in this unit group.
Marine Emergency Duties Certificate is required by Transport Canada.
A valid Fishing Vessel Operator Certificate is required for all occupations in this unit group.
A commercial fishing licence is required.
additional_information: Completion of Transport Canada examinations is required to advance from Fishing Master Class IV through Class I. Training programs are provided in provincial training centres.
Progression to merchant or government vessel officer positions (which require a Marine Officer Certificate) is possible with additional training and experience.

core_competencies.statement: to navigate the vessel, deploy fishing gear and manage catches efficiently and safely

core_competencies.competency: Critical Thinking, Decision Making and Problem Solving
core_competencies.statement: to evaluate complex situations that impact the safety and success of fishing operations, such as changing sea conditions or unexpected equipment failures, in order to make informed decisions
to adjust the speed and force of fishing equipment to optimize catch rates and respond to changing conditions, such as sudden shifts in weather or unexpected movements of fish
to accurately assess underwater conditions, such as the depth of fishing nets or fish schools
to make critical decisions and manage operations effectively in often remote and isolated environments

core_competencies.competency: Knowledge in Livestock, Farm Animals & Wildlife
core_competencies.statement: to understand fish activities to target specific fish, implementing sustainable practices and comply with regulations

units.title: Fishermen/women
units.category: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.broad_category_title: Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupations
units.major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and related production
units.sub_major_group_title: Occupations in natural resources and fisheries
units.definition: Fishermen/women operate fishing vessels less than 100 gross tonnes to pursue and land fish and other marine life. They are usually self-employed owner-operators of fishing vessels.
units.metric_source_note: StatCan annual labour-force and wage tables do not publish this official NOC 2021 unit group directly. Labour-market counts are estimated by allocating the published StatCan annual occupation tables across official unit groups using ESDC outlook employment weights. Rates and wage fields inherit published source-group values until a direct canonical StatCan release is added.

oasis_profiles.lead_statement: Fishermen/women operate fishing vessels less than 100 gross tonnes to pursue and land fish and other marine life.
employment_requirements: Several years of experience as fishing vessel crew member or helper are usually required.
Marine Emergency Duties Certificate is required by Transport Canada.
A valid Fishing Vessel Operator Certificate is required.
A commercial fishing licence is required.
Licences are required for each species of fish pursued.
Trade certification for fish harvesters is available, but voluntary, in Newfoundland and Labrador.
additional_information: Progression to master or mate on fishing vessels over 100 gross tonnes is possible with additional training, licensing and experience.

core_competencies.statement: to navigate the vessel, deploy fishing gear and manage catches efficiently
to maintain the vessel and equipment by regularly checking and servicing equipment to prevent breakdowns and ensure reliability
to spot and track marine life, navigate safely and detect potential hazards from a distance
to handle and move heavy fishing gear and equipment, such as nets and lines that may contain fish
to adapt to changing conditions at sea, manage tasks effectively without constant supervision and maintaining self-sufficiency during extended periods away from shore

core_competencies.competency: Knowledge in Livestock, Farm Animals & Wildlife
core_competencies.statement: to understand fish activities to target specific fish, comply with fishing regulations and prevent overfishing of species
